ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indomie Caksu with egg. FOODON_03315595 (instant noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class. The caksu sauce and egg components are not individually represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodles, instant, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this instant noodle dish with egg and sauce additions.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers cooked instant noodles as a category. The caksu sauce (sweet soy and sambal) and egg toppings are not separately coded; this is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific dish or its closest analogue. SNOMED CT does not maintain a food composition hierarchy suitable for this dish.

main_ingredients: caksu sauce (sweet soy sauce / kecap manis)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Viral Eggy Indomie Caksu is approximately 350 g, comprising one pack of cooked Indomie instant noodles (approximately 85 g dry weight), two eggs, and a generous portion of caksu sauce and sambal.

nutrient_source.title: Indomie Mi Goreng Instant Noodles — nutrient composition adapted with egg and sauce additions; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked instant noodles and whole egg
nutrient_source.organization: Singapore HPB Foodo Database; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Viral Eggy Indomie Caksu' exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from: (1) HPB Foodo entry for Indomie Mi Goreng (cooked, per 100g), (2) USDA FoodData Central entry for whole egg (NDB 01123), and (3) standard kecap manis and sambal sauce composition from HPB Foodo condiment entries. The input record reported energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7g, fat 0.0g, carbohydrate 2.36g per 100g — these values are internally inconsistent and implausible for this dish composition and have been replaced with estimated composite values documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Instant noodles, white wheat flour base — GI value 73 (analogous estimate); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for Indomie Caksu with egg. GI of 73 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white wheat flour instant noodles (cooked), which is the closest defensible analogue. The caksu sauce (kecap manis) adds sugar but the overall dish GI is dominated by the refined wheat noodle base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (73) × available carbohydrate per serving (33g at 350g serving weight) / 100 = 24.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~450 mg/100g) from Indomie seasoning packet and caksu sauce, with insufficient potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish; if consumed, use only half the Indomie seasoning packet and request reduced caksu sauce to lower sodium intake, and pair with a high-potassium side such as steamed vegetables.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 73) from refined wheat instant noodle base combined with added sugar from kecap manis caksu sauce, driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high GI and moderate glycemic load (GL ~24 per serving); substituting konjac or whole-grain noodles and requesting reduced caksu sauce can meaningfully lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (~450 mg/100g), moderate saturated fat from egg yolks and palm oil in Indomie seasoning, and ultra-processed food classification
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally; the high sodium content raises blood pressure risk, and the ultra-processed instant noodle base is associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes in epidemiological studies — choose lower-sodium alternatives and increase vegetable accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrate base, high sodium from seasoning, added sugar from caksu sauce, chili as a nightshade vegetable, and AGEs from fried shallots
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; the refined noodle base and high sodium promote inflammatory mediators, chili may trigger flares in nightshade-sensitive individuals, and no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, cruciferous vegetables) are present to offset these risks.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Viral Eggy Indomie Caksu is a popular Indonesian-origin instant noodle dish that gained widespread social media attention in Singapore, accumulating over 10 million views on TikTok. The dish is built on a base of Indomie instant noodles — a refined wheat flour product — cooked and tossed with two eggs (typically pan-fried or scrambled directly into the noodles), the proprietary Indomie seasoning packet, and a generous serving of caksu sauce. Caksu sauce is a sweet, thick condiment made primarily from kecap manis (Indonesian sweet soy sauce) combined with sambal chili paste, creating a sweet-spicy-savoury flavour profile. The dish is typically garnished with fried shallots (bawang goreng), which add crunch and a mild caramelised onion flavour. Texturally, the noodles are soft and slightly chewy, coated in a glossy, sticky sauce with pockets of egg providing richness and protein. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 148 kcal per 100g, with moderate protein from the eggs (~6.2g/100g), moderate fat (~5.8g/100g) from egg yolks and palm oil in the seasoning, and a significant carbohydrate load (~18.5g/100g) dominated by the refined wheat noodle base. Sodium is high at approximately 450 mg/100g, making the dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is high (GI ~73), driven by the refined wheat noodle base and added sugar from kecap manis, posing a significant postprandial glucose risk for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is classified as ultra-processed due to the instant noodle base and commercial seasoning packet. It is halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains gluten (wheat) and egg allergens. Known in Chinese as 鸡蛋印度美叉苏面, in Malay as Indomie Caksu Telur Viral, and in Indonesian as Indomie Caksu Telur. Best consumed as an occasional treat rather than a regular meal component.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Viral Eggy Indomie Caksu' exists in any approved primary source (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from composite dish components (Indomie Mi Goreng base from HPB Foodo, whole egg from USDA SR Legacy, kecap manis and sambal from HPB Foodo condiment entries). The input record's per-100g values (energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7g, fat 0.0g, carbohydrate 2.36g) were internally inconsistent and implausible for this dish composition and have been replaced with estimated composite values. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: INPUT DATA ISSUES: The input record reported per-100g values of energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7g, fat 0.0g, carbohydrate 2.36g. These values are internally inconsistent: 10.7g protein × 4 kcal/g + 0g fat × 9 kcal/g + 2.36g carbohydrate × 4 kcal/g = 52.3 kcal, not 55 kcal. More critically, fat_g of 0.0g is implausible for a dish containing eggs (which contribute ~5g fat per egg) and fried shallots. The input values appear to correspond to a different food item and have been discarded. REVISED ESTIMATES: Per-100g values are estimated from a composite dish of approximately 350g total serving weight comprising: one pack Indomie Mi Goreng (85g dry, ~170g cooked), two whole eggs (~100g), caksu sauce (~60g kecap manis and sambal blend), and fried shallots (~20g). HPB Foodo Indomie Mi Goreng cooked values and USDA FoodData Central NDB 01123 (whole egg, raw) were used as primary component sources. ZERO/NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: No core macro is zero in the revised estimates. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4), trans_fat_g (0.1) are estimated from egg and palm oil fatty acid profiles in USDA SR Legacy; these are estimates and carry moderate uncertainty. SODIUM: Sodium estimate of 450 mg/100g is consistent with the input record's health_suitability notes and is supported by HPB Foodo Indomie Mi Goreng sodium data (~800mg per pack) diluted across the full 350g serving. GI: GI of 73 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white wheat flour instant noodles; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for this dish. MULTILINGUAL: This dish is Indonesian in origin but is consumed and marketed in Singapore. The validator requires Singaporean multilingual coverage (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) due to the SG region tag. Chinese simplified name 鸡蛋印度美叉苏面 (Jīdàn Yìndùměi Chāsū Miàn) is a descriptive transliteration meaning 'egg Indomie caksu noodles'. Chinese traditional form 雞蛋印度美叉蘇麵 is provided. Malay name 'Indomie Caksu Telur Viral' uses the Indonesian/Malay cognate form. Tamil name வைரல் முட்டை இண்டோமி காக்சு is a phonetic rendering. Hindi name वायरल एगी इंडोमी काक्सू is a phonetic rendering. These names are best-effort transliterations as no established native-script names exist for this social-media-viral dish in these languages.
